text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY AND BREAST SURGERY
PRE-OPERATIVE ASSESSMENT — COMBINED CLINIC VISIT

PATIENT: Marisol Fuentes-Delgado
DOB: [on file]
MRN: [on file]
ATTENDING (Oncology): [Attending Oncologist]
ATTENDING (Breast Surgery): [Attending Breast Surgeon]
VISIT TYPE: Pre-operative combined oncology/surgical assessment

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Fuentes-Delgado is a 58-year-old Hispanic postmenopausal woman with germline BRCA1-mutated, stage IIB (T2N1M0) triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) who presents today for a combined pre-operative assessment with medical oncology and breast surgery following completion of neoadjuvant systemic therapy. She is being evaluated for planned left modified radical mastectomy, contralateral prophylactic mastectomy (CPM), and sentinel lymph node biopsy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Ms. Fuentes-Delgado was initially diagnosed with left breast invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, triple-negative (ER-/PR-/HER2-), basal-like subtype approximately six months ago. Staging at the time of diagnosis revealed T2N1M0 disease (stage IIB per AJCC 8th edition) based on a 3.4 cm primary mass at the 12 o'clock position and a single clinically suspicious left axillary lymph node confirmed by ultrasound-guided FNA. She was found to carry a germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC), and pre-treatment NGS of her tumor showed a high HRD score of 62, MYC amplification, PTEN deletion, RB1 loss, and CDKN2A deletion alongside the expected TP53 hotspot mutation (p.R248W). PD-L1 CPS was 28 by 22C3. Given these features, she was treated per the KEYNOTE-522 framework with neoadjuvant pembro (Keytruda) combined with dose-dense doxo/cyclo (ddAC) followed by weekly Taxol.

Neoadjuvant therapy course summary:
- pembro 200 mg IV q3 weeks — completed 8 cycles total (neoadjuvant phase)
- ddAC (doxo 60 mg/m2 + cyclo 600 mg/m2 q2 weeks x 4 cycles) — completed; notable toxicities included grade 3 neutropenia after cycle 1 (managed with G-CSF), grade 2 fatigue and nausea, and grade 1 alopecia (total)
- Weekly pacli 80 mg/m2 for 12 weeks concurrent with Keytruda — complicated by grade 3 peripheral sensory neuropathy, prompting dose reduction to 65 mg/m2 for the final 4 weeks; duloxetine initiated for neuropathic symptom management
- Immune-related adverse events: grade 2 hypothyroidism (Keytruda-attributed, TSH 18.4 at onset; levothyroxine initiated and currently continued), grade 2 arthralgia (bilateral knees and wrists, managed with scheduled ibuprofen)

Mid-neoadjuvant MRI (after cycle 4 of ddAC/pembro) demonstrated partial radiographic response: residual enhancing mass 1.6 x 1.4 cm, down from 3.4 cm baseline (approximately 53% size reduction). The suspicious left axillary lymph node also showed partial treatment response with partial restoration of fatty hilum.

She completed her final dose of PTX approximately two weeks ago. Her last Keytruda infusion was given three weeks prior to today's visit as scheduled. She now presents for pre-operative evaluation. Surgery is planned in the next 1-2 weeks pending clearance.

Regarding the contralateral prophylactic mastectomy: Ms. Fuentes-Delgado has been counseled extensively regarding her germline BRCA1 carrier status, her estimated lifetime risk of contralateral breast cancer (approximately 50-60% over 10 years in BRCA1 carriers), and the risk-reduction benefits of CPM. After discussion with oncology, breast surgery, and genetic counseling, she has elected to proceed with simultaneous bilateral mastectomy with immediate tissue expander placement for staged reconstruction. She understands this is a personal risk-reduction decision and that CPM has not been shown to improve survival in the metastatic setting, but that it substantially reduces the risk of a new primary breast cancer.

The patient denies any new breast symptoms, skin changes, chest wall pain, or arm swelling since her last visit. She continues to experience residual peripheral neuropathy (fingertip tingling bilaterally) which has improved compared to its nadir. She reports fatigue that has been persistent but manageable. No shortness of breath, cough, or other new cardiopulmonary symptoms. No fever, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss. No bone pain or new musculoskeletal complaints beyond her known bilateral knee osteoarthritis.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:

Constitutional: Persistent grade 2 fatigue; no fever, chills, or unintentional weight loss; appetite fair to good.
Neurological: Grade 1-2 peripheral sensory neuropathy (bilateral fingertips and toes, improved from prior grade 3 nadir; duloxetine helpful); no new neurological complaints.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, dyspnea at rest, or lower extremity edema. Mild exertional dyspnea noted with climbing stairs; she attributes this to deconditioning. Blood pressure has been intermittently elevated; on increased lisinopril dose since mid-treatment.
Pulmonary: No cough, wheezing, or shortness of breath at rest.
Gastrointestinal: Appetite returned to baseline over the past 2-3 weeks. No nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea currently. Mild residual constipation managed with dietary modifications and MiraLAX as needed.
Musculoskeletal: Grade 1-2 arthralgia in bilateral knees and wrists (ongoing from immune-related arthritis vs. baseline osteoarthritis); stable. Right shoulder mildly sore (patient attributes to positioning).
Skin/integument: Alopecia nearly complete (total). Grade 1 nail changes — onycholysis of both great toes, stable. No new rashes or skin breakdown.
Endocrine: Hypothyroidism currently stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; last TSH was within normal range. Diabetes — glycemic control improved per endocrinology management; last HbA1c 7.6%.
Psychiatric/mood: Patient endorses baseline anxiety related to upcoming surgery and overall cancer diagnosis. She feels "ready" for surgery and expresses cautious optimism. Coping with family support.
Genitourinary/GYN: Postmenopausal; no vaginal bleeding. No dysuria.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Triple-negative breast cancer, left breast, stage IIB (T2N1M0) — see HPI
2. Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC, p.Gln1756Profs*74)
3. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — on metformin; co-managed with endocrinology; HbA1c 7.6% (improved from 8.4% at diagnosis)
4. Hypertension — on lisinopril (dose increased during treatment)
5. Obesity (BMI 33.2 at time of diagnosis; current BMI approximately 31.8 after treatment-related weight changes)
6. Immune-related hypothyroidism (Keytruda-attributed) — on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily
7. Bilateral knee osteoarthritis — on ibuprofen PRN/scheduled
8. Peripheral sensory neuropathy (treatment-related, Taxol) — on duloxetine 30 mg daily

PAST SURGICAL/PROCEDURAL HISTORY:
- Ultrasound-guided core needle biopsy, left breast (diagnostic)
- Ultrasound-guided FNA, left axillary lymph node (diagnostic)
- Port-a-cath placement (for chemotherapy administration)
- Port-a-cath is currently in place; plan for removal at time of mastectomy or post-operatively

FAMILY HISTORY:
- Mother: breast cancer diagnosed at age 42 (likely BRCA1-related)
- Maternal aunt: ovarian cancer (likely BRCA1-related)
- No known colorectal, pancreatic, or prostate cancers in first-degree relatives
- Genetic counseling completed; family members informed of BRCA1 variant in the family

SOCIAL HISTORY:
- Former smoker: 10 pack-year history, quit approximately 15 years ago
- Alcohol: occasional social use, minimal during chemotherapy
- Illicit substances: none
- Occupation: works part-time as an administrative assistant; on medical leave during chemotherapy
- Lives with husband and adult daughter; strong family support system
- Spanish-speaking; English proficient; interpreter not required today
- Health literacy: good; engaged and health-literate; actively participates in decision-making

ALLERGIES:
- Penicillin — rash (remote, mechanism unclear; cross-reactivity considerations noted for surgical prophylaxis; anesthesia team aware)
- No known drug allergies to chemotherapy agents used in regimen

MEDICATIONS (current, at time of this visit):
1. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily (held on days of IV contrast/surgery per protocol)
2. Lisinopril 20 mg orally once daily
3. Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally once daily (take on empty stomach)
4. Duloxetine 30 mg orally once daily (neuropathic pain/mood support)
5. Ibuprofen 400 mg orally three times daily with food (immune-related and osteoarthritis-related arthralgia; held perioperatively)
6. Ondansetron 8 mg orally as needed (for residual nausea; not currently using daily)
7. MiraLAX (polyethylene glycol 3350) 17 g orally as needed for constipation
8. Vitamin D3 2000 IU orally daily
9. Filgrastim — completed (was used for G-CSF support; no longer active)
10. Fosaprepitant — completed (antiemetic during AC; no longer active)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 138/86 mmHg (improved from earlier treatment period), heart rate 78 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, temperature 36.8°C, oxygen saturation 97% on room air, weight [on file], BMI approximately 31.8.

General: Alert and oriented, pleasant Hispanic woman in no acute distress. Well-groomed despite ongoing alopecia. Affect appropriate; appears fatigued but engaged and motivated.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Complete alopecia (chemotherapy-related). No icterus. No cervical, supraclavicular, or infraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpated bilaterally. Oral mucosa moist; mild residual oral hyperpigmentation at lower lip (chemotherapy-related, benign).

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; normal S1 and S2; no S3 or S4 gallop; no murmurs. No jugular venous distension. No lower extremity pitting edema. Peripheral pulses 2+ and equal bilaterally.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally; no wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.

Breast Examination:
- Left breast: Total alopecia. Small area of residual palpable firmness/induration at the 12 o'clock position, approximately 4 cm from the nipple, estimated at less than 1.5 cm in greatest dimension. This is most consistent with post-treatment fibrosis versus small volume residual tumor; the area is firm and irregular but significantly smaller than the original 3.4 cm mass. There is no overlying skin erythema, dimpling, peau d'orange, or ulceration. The nipple is retracted slightly (pre-existing).
- Left axilla: No palpable lymphadenopathy. The previously palpable suspicious lymph node (1.8 cm at presentation) is no longer palpable.
- Right breast: No dominant mass, no skin changes, no nipple discharge. No palpable right axillary lymphadenopathy.
- Port-a-cath: Right chest wall; site intact, no tenderness or signs of infection.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly on palpation. No periumbilical or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Musculoskeletal: Bilateral mild knee crepitus; no significant joint effusion. Bilateral wrist mild tenderness on deep palpation (stable from prior). Gait slightly antalgic related to bilateral knee arthritis and peripheral neuropathy.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Distal extremity sensory testing: decreased light touch sensation at bilateral fingertips (grade 1-2 neuropathy, improved from nadir) and bilateral toes. DTRs 2+ upper extremities, 1+ bilateral ankles. Motor strength 5/5 throughout.

Skin: Complete scalp alopecia, diffuse. Onycholysis bilateral great toes (grade 1, stable). No active rashes. No hyperpigmentation at injection/infusion sites of clinical concern. No skin metastases.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (most recent, within past 2 weeks):

CBC: WBC 4.8 x10^9/L, ANC 2.6 x10^9/L (recovered from nadir, adequate), Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL (mild anemia, grade 1; stable), Platelets 228 x10^9/L
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Na 138 mEq/L, K 4.1 mEq/L, Cl 101 mEq/L, CO2 24 mEq/L, BUN 18 mg/dL, Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (eGFR ~52 mL/min, stable chronic kidney disease), glucose 128 mg/dL (fasting)
Liver function tests: AST 22 U/L, ALT 28 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 74 U/L, Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, Albumin 3.8 g/dL — all within normal limits
Thyroid function: TSH 3.2 mU/L (normal range; stable on levothyroxine)
HbA1c: 7.6% (improved, glycemic control adequate for perioperative planning)
Coagulation: PT/INR 1.0, PTT 28 seconds — within normal limits
Type and screen: Pending/ordered for surgical preparation

Echocardiogram (most recent, approximately 2 weeks ago):
Left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF): 58% (down from baseline 62% prior to treatment). No regional wall motion abnormalities. No pericardial effusion. Mild diastolic dysfunction grade I. This represents a mild decline from baseline, consistent with subclinical anthracycline cardiotoxicity. No clinical signs of heart failure. Cardiology has been notified and is following the patient; surveillance echocardiogram planned post-operatively. Cardiology team has cleared patient for surgery with recommendation for ongoing monitoring.

---

IMAGING RESULTS (most relevant recent):

Breast MRI (mid-neoadjuvant, after 4 cycles ddAC/pembro): Residual enhancing left breast mass 1.6 x 1.4 cm, down from 3.4 cm baseline; partial radiographic response, approximately 53% reduction. Left axillary node with partial treatment response. No new lesions. No repeat MRI today given timeline to surgery; clinical exam and prior MRI data guide surgical planning.

Staging CT (baseline): No distant metastases; locoregional disease only.

Bone scan (baseline): No osseous metastases; bilateral knee degenerative changes.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. TRIPLE-NEGATIVE BREAST CANCER, LEFT BREAST, STAGE IIB (T2N1M0), GERMLINE BRCA1-MUTATED — STATUS POST NEOADJUVANT THERAPY, NOW PRE-OPERATIVE:

Ms. Fuentes-Delgado has completed her planned neoadjuvant regimen: 8 cycles of Keytruda (Keytruda) concurrent with 4 cycles of ddAC followed by 12 weeks of weekly pacli (dose-reduced in last 4 weeks to 65 mg/m2 due to grade 3 neuropathy). She has achieved a partial clinical and radiographic response — the left breast mass has decreased substantially from 3.4 cm to estimated less than 1.5 cm palpable firmness (consistent with MRI showing 1.6 cm residual at mid-neoadjuvant assessment), and the previously suspicious axillary lymph node is no longer palpable. It is unlikely she has achieved a complete pathologic response given persistent residual induration and ongoing radiographic residual enhancement; however, pathologic response will be definitively determined at surgery.

She is now medically cleared for the planned surgical intervention:
- Left modified radical mastectomy with immediate tissue expander placement for reconstruction
- Right contralateral prophylactic mastectomy with immediate tissue expander placement
- Sentinel lymph node biopsy, left axilla (plan for 3-4 sentinel nodes)
- Port-a-cath removal will be coordinated with the surgical team

Perioperative considerations:
- Penicillin allergy on file (rash); anesthesia and surgical teams briefed; alternative antibiotic prophylaxis (clindamycin or cefazolin depending on allergy re-evaluation) to be coordinated per anesthesia
- Metformin held starting the night before surgery
- Ibuprofen held 5 days pre-operatively
- Lisinopril held morning of surgery per anesthesia protocol
- Levothyroxine should be taken morning of surgery with a small sip of water
- Duloxetine continued through perioperative period
- DVT prophylaxis with sequential compression devices intraoperatively; post-operative LMWH if no bleeding concerns per surgical team
- Blood pressure monitoring closely post-operatively given hypertension history
- Given CrCl ~52 mL/min, renal function will be monitored; IV contrast and nephrotoxic medications avoided if possible in perioperative period

2. CARDIAC FUNCTION MONITORING — MILD LVEF DECLINE (58% from 62% baseline):
Subclinical anthracycline-related cardiotoxicity. No clinical heart failure. Patient is asymptomatic from a cardiac standpoint. Cardiology cleared for surgery. Surveillance echocardiogram to be scheduled 3 months post-operatively. ACE inhibitor (lisinopril) serves dual purpose of hypertension management and cardiac protection (continued). No new cardiac medications initiated at this time. If LVEF declines further on surveillance, cardiology/oncology discussion regarding heart failure prophylaxis will be initiated.

3. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY (PTX-RELATED, GRADE 1-2 AT THIS VISIT, IMPROVED FROM GRADE 3 NADIR):
Currently grade 1 at fingertips; patient reports significant improvement with duloxetine and Taxol dose reduction. Continue duloxetine 30 mg daily. Monitor for any changes post-operatively; if neuropathy worsens, neurology follow-up to be arranged. No dose modifications needed at this time. Full recovery may take months to a year or more.

4. IMMUNE-RELATED HYPOTHYROIDISM (Keytruda-ATTRIBUTED):
Currently well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; TSH 3.2 mU/L (normal). Continue levothyroxine. Thyroid function to be rechecked at 6-8 week post-operative follow-up. If adjuvant Keytruda is resumed post-operatively (per KEYNOTE-522 adjuvant protocol for approximately 9 remaining cycles), close thyroid monitoring will be maintained.

5. IMMUNE-RELATED ARTHRALGIA (GRADE 1-2, STABLE):
Current management with scheduled ibuprofen 400 mg TID, which will be held perioperatively. Consider switching to acetaminophen for pain management post-operatively given renal function concerns and perioperative NSAID restrictions. If arthralgia flares post-operatively, rheumatology co-management may be considered. The role of ongoing Keytruda in maintaining this adverse event will be discussed at adjuvant therapy initiation visit.

6. TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS:
Glycemic control improved (HbA1c 7.6%). Metformin held perioperatively. Glucose monitoring post-operatively; sliding scale insulin per surgical team if needed. Endocrinology to follow up in outpatient setting after discharge. Goal HbA1c < 7.5% for optimal surgical wound healing.

7. HYPERTENSION:
Blood pressure 138/86 mmHg today — improved from earlier treatment period highs. Lisinopril dose increased previously and is providing adequate control. Lisinopril held morning of surgery; resume post-operatively once oral intake established and hemodynamically stable. Monitor closely in post-op period.

8. GERMLINE BRCA1 STATUS AND CONTRALATERAL RISK REDUCTION:
Decision to undergo simultaneous CPM has been made by the patient after full informed consent discussion. CPM will be performed at the time of the left MRM. Patient has also been counseled regarding BRCA1-related risk of ovarian cancer (estimated lifetime risk ~44%). Gynecologic oncology referral is recommended for consideration of risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO) in the future (typically recommended by age 35-40 in BRCA1 carriers; in this patient who is postmenopausal, RRSO remains beneficial for ovarian cancer risk reduction). Referral placed today.

9. ADJUVANT THERAPY PLANNING (POST-SURGERY, PER DISCUSSION):
Pathology results from the surgical specimen will determine extent of residual disease and RCB classification. Given the partial radiographic response and persistent palpable abnormality, it is anticipated (though not confirmed) that there will be residual invasive disease at surgery — i.e., she will not have a complete pathologic response (pCR). If RCB I-II or greater is confirmed:
- Adjuvant Xeloda (per CREATE-X protocol) will be initiated after wound healing, typically 4-6 weeks post-operatively
- Adjuvant Keytruda continuation (per KEYNOTE-522, completing up to 1 year of total pembro) will be discussed; approximately 9 cycles remain in the adjuvant phase
- Given germline BRCA1 mutation, adjuvant ola (OlympiA data: ~8.8% absolute 4-year iDFS benefit in germline BRCA1/2 HER2-negative early breast cancer) will be offered; patient has previously expressed hesitancy given peripheral neuropathy and polypharmacy burden. This decision will be revisited at the post-operative oncology visit once pathology is available and toxicity has had further time to resolve.
- Radiation oncology referral will be placed for post-mastectomy radiation therapy (PMRT) if there is residual nodal involvement at surgery (currently anticipated given partial response of the axillary node).

10. SURVEILLANCE AND FOLLOW-UP PLANNING:
- Surgery scheduled: within the next 1-2 weeks (coordinated with breast surgery team and anesthesia)
- Post-operative oncology visit: approximately 3 weeks after surgery for wound check, pathology review, and adjuvant therapy planning
- Radiation oncology consultation: to be placed now, referral submitted today
- Cardiology surveillance echocardiogram: ~3 months post-operatively
- Endocrinology: ongoing co-management for diabetes and hypothyroidism
- Gynecologic oncology: referral for RRSO counseling submitted today
- Plastic/reconstructive surgery: tissue expander follow-up; planned implant exchange in approximately 6 months after radiation therapy completion (given planned PMRT, implant exchange will be deferred until after radiation to optimize cosmetic outcomes and reduce capsular contracture risk)

Patient and family were present for this visit and all discussed above. Extensive time spent discussing surgical plan, reconstruction, adjuvant therapy options, and perioperative risk optimization. Patient expresses understanding and agreement with the plan. She reports feeling emotionally supported by her husband and daughter. Questions answered in full. Consent for surgical procedures to be obtained by the surgical team at the pre-operative surgical appointment.

ECOG Performance Status: 1
Cancer Status: Residual disease present (partial clinical/radiographic response to neoadjuvant therapy; pCR not achieved); pre-operative, not yet in surgical remission.

Return to clinic: Post-operatively, approximately 3 weeks after surgery.

Documented by: [Attending Oncologist], MD, Hematology/Oncology
Co-evaluated by: [Attending Breast Surgeon], MD, Breast Surgery
Note reviewed and signed by attending.
